teaching_arc.entry_point: Students already know basic graphing and algebra, but may not yet connect formula restrictions, graph shapes, and transformations as one system.
teaching_arc.progression: Start with the function-machine idea, review families, then build tools in order: domain and range, transformations, composition, symmetry, and piecewise behavior.
teaching_arc.destination: Students can inspect a formula or graph and say what family it belongs to, what inputs are allowed, how it moved, and what visual clue matters.
teaching_arc.tone: Clear, warm, visual, active, and personal-brand friendly.
methods: Use interactive plots as the main explanation surface.
Use short worked steps for examples so students can follow without a wall of text.
Name common mistakes before they become habits.
During narrated playback, keep widgets moving at meaningful moments: sliders, highlighted points, or clicked solution steps should change when the instructor discusses them.

audience_level: first-year university and advanced high-school students learning calculus through visual intuition, formulas, and guided practice
topic_goal: Review functions and graphs as the visual language students need before limits, derivatives, and integrals.
topic_takeaways: Recognize the main function families by both formula and graph shape.
Find domain and range by asking which inputs are allowed and what outputs can appear.
Apply shifts, stretches, and reflections in a deliberate order without the horizontal-sign mistake.
Compose functions while carrying both the inner-function and outer-function domain restrictions.
Use symmetry and piecewise boundary behavior to reason from formulas and graphs together.
style_notes: The instructor is Arian from Arian University; the first slide should introduce that identity naturally and only once.
Use short visible chunks as narration anchors; do not read every bullet verbatim.
Move between formula, graph, and plain-language interpretation on each example.
When a widget is present, make the video feel active: call out the parameter or highlighted point, then let the scripted timeline or step state move while explaining.
For website mode, leave students with a reason to drag the controls themselves after the narrated explanation.
On dense visual slides, keep the avatar low and out of the main math area so the slide carries focus.
For pause-style questions, give students a real beat, then answer with one concrete test point or graph feature.
transition_style: Use each slide as one teaching beat: name the task, point to the visual or formula, move the interactive state when useful, then preview the next tool.

slides.narration_seed: Open with the counterintuitive horizontal-shift question, then anchor Review of Functions and Graphs as the first topic in Session 1: Preliminaries of the Calculus 1 series, then introduce Arian University once.
must_cover: Series anchor: Review of Functions and Graphs is the first topic in Session 1, Preliminaries, of Calculus 1.
This is a review, but it is not filler: every tool returns in limits, derivatives, and integrals.
Students should listen for the connection between formula, graph, and allowed inputs.
The session will stay visual, interactive, and example-driven.
slides.transition_from_previous: Open by framing the topic goal: Help students understand review of functions and graphs in a way they can explain, apply, and build on later.
slides.transition_to_next: Now that the destination is clear, show the exact skills students should be able to do by the end.
slides.likely_student_confusion: Students may think review means optional. Make it feel like sharpening the tools before calculus starts.
slides.explanation_style: Start personally and directly, then move quickly into the purpose of the session.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should feel curiosity in the first 15 seconds and understand where this topic sits in the full Calculus 1 path.
must_say: Quick prediction: why does f of x plus two move a graph left, not right?
Hi, I'm Arian, and welcome to Arian University.
This first toolkit video shows how to read a function from its formula, graph, and restrictions.
slides.scene_hint: Start with the horizontal-shift sign puzzle as the hook, then place the video inside the Calculus 1 series before introducing Arian University.

slides.narration_seed: Use the roadmap first at the session level: five sessions total, current Session 1 is Preliminaries, next Session 2 is Limits and Continuity, and this topic builds the graph language that limits assume. Then keep the objectives short and visual.
must_cover: Use the course roadmap at session level: five sessions total, current Session 1 Preliminaries, next Session 2 Limits and Continuity.
Do not say topic X of Y out loud; the numbered roadmap boxes show exact progress visually.
By the end, formulas should feel like visual instructions.
Inputs, graph movement, composition restrictions, symmetry, and piecewise boundaries are the main checklist.
slides.transition_from_previous: The title named the session; this slide turns it into a promise.
slides.transition_to_next: Begin with the simplest model of a function: one input goes in, one output comes out.
slides.likely_student_confusion: Students may see too many skills at once. Tell them the lecture will build them one at a time.
slides.explanation_style: Use the visible chunks as anchors, then explain in plain language with one concrete graph or formula reference.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should see where this topic sits in the Calculus 1 path and know the payoff without feeling trapped in a long checklist.

slides.narration_seed: Use the vending machine analogy only as a quick foundation, then pivot back to visual graph action.
must_cover: A function assigns each valid input exactly one output.
Domain means the inputs that work.
Range means the outputs that can appear.
Transformations move or rescale the machine without changing the one-input-one-output rule.
slides.transition_from_previous: The objectives are abstract; this analogy makes the first words tangible.
slides.transition_to_next: Once students know what a function does, show the main families they will recognize again and again.
slides.likely_student_confusion: Do not let the analogy become too long. Use it to define input, output, domain, and range, then move on.
slides.explanation_style: Use the visible chunks as anchors, then explain in plain language with one concrete graph or formula reference.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should get input, output, domain, and range without losing momentum.
slides.scene_hint: No slider is needed here; use the image actively by naming button, input, output, domain, and range in sequence.

slides.narration_seed: Treat the eight-family slide as a fast montage under a minute, with one real-world sine frequency moment and a quick handoff to domain.
must_cover: Linear and quadratic functions are the baseline shapes for lines and parabolas.
Polynomial and power functions generalize familiar algebraic shapes.
Rational, logarithmic, and square-root style functions often create domain restrictions.
Trigonometric, exponential, and logarithmic families will return throughout calculus.
slides.transition_from_previous: The vending-machine model defines a function; the gallery shows the common machines we meet.
slides.transition_to_next: Before transforming graphs, first ask where a function is allowed to exist.
slides.likely_student_confusion: Students may try to copy all formulas. Emphasize pattern recognition and shape-reading.
slides.explanation_style: Use the visible chunks as anchors, then explain in plain language with one concrete graph or formula reference.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should recognize families fast without feeling the video has become a formula catalog.
slides.scene_hint: Use the family gallery like a quick visual scan: pause briefly on each graph family and name the signature shape.

slides.narration_seed: Walk this example slowly: the square root forces the radicand to be nonnegative, then the +2 lifts the lowest output.
must_cover: For \(f(x)=\sqrt{x-3}+2\), require \(x-3\ge 0\), so \(x\ge 3\).
The smallest input is \(x=3\), and it gives \(f(3)=2\).
As \(x\) increases, \(\sqrt{x-3}\) increases, so the range is \([2,\infty)\).
Use the graph endpoint to confirm the algebra.
slides.transition_from_previous: The family gallery hinted at restrictions; this example turns that hint into a method.
slides.transition_to_next: Now that the allowed inputs are clear, show how graphs move when formulas change.
slides.likely_student_confusion: Students may say the range starts at 0 because of the square root parent. Point to the vertical shift.
slides.explanation_style: Use short text chunks as anchors, but teach by moving the visual state and pointing to one concrete formula or graph feature.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should see the graph or formula change at the exact moment the spoken explanation names the rule.
slides.scene_hint: Use the moving point on the square-root graph: start at the endpoint, move right as x increases, and connect that motion to range.

slides.narration_seed: Separate vertical shifts from horizontal shifts. Vertical changes are direct; horizontal changes require solving the inside expression.
must_cover: \(y=f(x)+c\) moves the graph up when \(c>0\) and down when \(c<0\).
\(y=f(x-c)\) moves the graph right when \(c>0\) and left when \(c<0\).
The horizontal sign feels opposite because the input must solve \(x-c=0\).
Use one anchor point on the graph to verify the movement.
slides.transition_from_previous: Domain told us where the graph can live; transformations tell us where the whole graph moves.
slides.transition_to_next: After shifts, change the graph size and orientation with stretches and reflections.
slides.likely_student_confusion: Horizontal shifts are the main sign trap. Repeat the anchor-point test, not only the rule.
slides.explanation_style: Use short text chunks as anchors, but teach by moving the visual state and pointing to one concrete formula or graph feature.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should see the graph or formula change at the exact moment the spoken explanation names the rule.
slides.scene_hint: Move the transformation controls while speaking: first vertical shift, then horizontal shift, and pause on the sign-opposite moment.

slides.narration_seed: Show vertical scaling as changing outputs and horizontal scaling as changing inputs. Use the sine period formula as the concrete anchor.
must_cover: \(c f(x)\) scales outputs: vertical stretch, shrink, or reflection across the x-axis.
\(f(cx)\) scales inputs: horizontal compression or stretch, and negative \(c\) reflects across the y-axis.
For \(\sin(bx)\), the period is \(2\pi/|b|\). Larger \(|b|\) means a shorter period.
Use the sliders to show amplitude first, then period, then reflection.
slides.transition_from_previous: Shifts move the graph; stretches and reflections reshape or flip it.
slides.transition_to_next: Before a full worked example, isolate the most common mistake about horizontal shifts.
slides.likely_student_confusion: Students may mix up vertical and horizontal scaling. Keep saying output scaling versus input scaling.
slides.explanation_style: Use short text chunks as anchors, but teach by moving the visual state and pointing to one concrete formula or graph feature.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should see the graph or formula change at the exact moment the spoken explanation names the rule.
slides.scene_hint: Move the stretch controls while speaking: amplitude first, period compression second, then reflection.

slides.narration_seed: Make this a warning slide: \(f(x+3)\) does not move right. Solve the inside equal to zero and the direction becomes left.
must_cover: The tempting mistake is reading \(x+3\) as “right 3.”
To find the moved anchor point, solve \(x+3=0\), giving \(x=-3\).
So \(f(x+3)\) shifts left 3.
Use the visual comparison to show the wrong and correct interpretations side by side.
slides.transition_from_previous: Scaling was already enough; this slide prevents the sign mistake that breaks many sketches.
slides.transition_to_next: Now combine shifts, stretches, and reflections in a full transformation sequence.
slides.likely_student_confusion: Students may remember the slogan but not why. Anchor it to the equation (x+3=0).
slides.explanation_style: Use short text chunks as anchors, but teach by moving the visual state and pointing to one concrete formula or graph feature.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should see the graph or formula change at the exact moment the spoken explanation names the rule.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual comparison as a correction: show the tempting right-shift idea, then move to the left-shift interpretation.

slides.narration_seed: Build \(y=-2(x+1)^2+3\) one step at a time from \(y=x^2\). Let each clickable step move the graph to the next state.
must_cover: Start from the parent parabola \(y=x^2\) with vertex \((0,0)\).
\((x+1)^2\) shifts the vertex left to \((-1,0)\).
The factor \(-2\) reflects across the x-axis and vertically stretches by 2.
The \(+3\) moves the vertex to \((-1,3)\).
slides.transition_from_previous: The caution slide told us the horizontal sign; this example uses that rule in context.
slides.transition_to_next: Next, instead of changing one function, feed one function into another.
slides.likely_student_confusion: Students may apply the vertical shift before the reflection and stretch. Emphasize the visible staged order.
slides.explanation_style: Use short text chunks as anchors, but teach by moving the visual state and pointing to one concrete formula or graph feature.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should see the graph or formula change at the exact moment the spoken explanation names the rule.
slides.scene_hint: Use the clickable/animated steps as the visual action: parent, left shift, reflect/stretch, then up shift.

slides.narration_seed: Explain composition as a pipeline: apply \(g\) first, then feed that output into \(f\). The domain must survive both stages.
must_cover: \((f\circ g)(x)=f(g(x))\): \(g\) happens first.
The input must be in the domain of \(g\).
The value \(g(x)\) must also be in the domain of \(f\).
Order matters: \(f\circ g\) and \(g\circ f\) are usually different.
slides.transition_from_previous: Transformations changed one graph; composition chains two functions together.
slides.transition_to_next: Now apply the domain rule to an example with a square root and a denominator.
slides.likely_student_confusion: Students read left-to-right and may apply (f) first. Say “inside first” and point to the pipeline.
slides.explanation_style: Use short text chunks as anchors, but teach by moving the visual state and pointing to one concrete formula or graph feature.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should see the graph or formula change at the exact moment the spoken explanation names the rule.
slides.scene_hint: Use the composition pipeline as an active path: input enters g first, output feeds f second, then domain checks wrap around both.

slide_ref.source_title: Example 3: Composition with a Hole in the Domain

slides.narration_seed: This example has two filters. The square root demands \(x\ge0\), and the denominator demands \(\sqrt{x}\ne1\), so \(x\ne1\).
must_cover: With \(f(x)=\frac{1}{x-1}\) and \(g(x)=\sqrt{x}\), the composition is \(f(g(x))=\frac{1}{\sqrt{x}-1}\).
The inner function requires \(x\ge0\).
The outer denominator requires \(\sqrt{x}-1\ne0\), so \(x\ne1\).
The final domain is \([0,1)\cup(1,\infty)\).
slides.transition_from_previous: The composition rule had two checks; this example shows why both checks matter.
slides.transition_to_next: After composition, shift to another graph-reading shortcut: symmetry.
slides.likely_student_confusion: Students may stop after (xge0) and miss the hole at (x=1). Point to the denominator and the graph marker.
slides.explanation_style: Use short text chunks as anchors, but teach by moving the visual state and pointing to one concrete formula or graph feature.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should see the graph or formula change at the exact moment the spoken explanation names the rule.
slides.scene_hint: Use the graph marker/domain highlight as each restriction is named: square-root restriction first, denominator hole second.

slides.narration_seed: Use the two plotted points to make symmetry physical: even means mirror across the y-axis, odd means rotate through the origin.
must_cover: Even functions satisfy \(f(-x)=f(x)\) and mirror across the y-axis.
Odd functions satisfy \(f(-x)=-f(x)\) and have origin symmetry.
The algebraic test is: substitute \(-x\), simplify, and compare.
Symmetry can simplify later calculus work, especially integrals.
slides.transition_from_previous: Composition asked how functions combine; symmetry asks how one function behaves at opposite inputs.
slides.transition_to_next: Now use the algebraic test on a rational function that is not obvious at first glance.
slides.likely_student_confusion: Students may think “odd function” means odd powers only. Use the definition as the final test.
slides.explanation_style: Use short text chunks as anchors, but teach by moving the visual state and pointing to one concrete formula or graph feature.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should see the graph or formula change at the exact moment the spoken explanation names the rule.
slides.scene_hint: Move paired points across the graph while explaining mirror symmetry and origin symmetry.

slides.narration_seed: Compute \(h(-x)\) carefully. The numerator changes sign, the denominator stays the same, so the whole function changes sign.
must_cover: For \(h(x)=\frac{x^3}{x^2+1}\), substitute \(-x\).
The numerator becomes \(-x^3\), while the denominator remains \(x^2+1\).
Therefore the function is odd and has origin symmetry.
slides.transition_from_previous: The symmetry definitions become useful when the graph is not immediately obvious.
slides.transition_to_next: Next, read a function whose rule changes depending on where (x) is.
slides.likely_student_confusion: Students may see a denominator and assume the symmetry is gone. Show that the denominator is even.
slides.explanation_style: Use short text chunks as anchors, but teach by moving the visual state and pointing to one concrete formula or graph feature.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should see the graph or formula change at the exact moment the spoken explanation names the rule.
slides.scene_hint: Move the highlighted test point from x to -x while simplifying h(-x), then point to the origin symmetry.

slides.narration_seed: For piecewise functions, first choose the correct condition, then evaluate. The open and closed dots decide what happens at boundaries.
must_cover: Always choose the piece by checking the condition on \(x\) first.
\(f(-1)=1\) from the \(x<0\) piece.
\(f(0)=1\) because the middle piece includes \(x=0\).
\(f(2)=5\) because the middle piece includes \(x=2\), while the right piece starts after 2.
There is a jump at \(x=0\) and a jump at \(x=2\).
slides.transition_from_previous: Symmetry gave one global test; piecewise functions require local decisions interval by interval.
slides.transition_to_next: End with a short proof challenge that uses the even and odd definitions directly.
slides.likely_student_confusion: Students may use the nearest-looking curve instead of the interval condition. Keep pointing to open versus closed dots.
slides.explanation_style: Use short text chunks as anchors, but teach by moving the visual state and pointing to one concrete formula or graph feature.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should see the graph or formula change at the exact moment the spoken explanation names the rule.
slides.scene_hint: Use the piecewise marker and boundary dots actively: select the interval first, then evaluate the point.

slides.narration_seed: Treat this as a short proof students can almost do in their heads. Multiply the definitions: even stays the same, odd changes sign, so the product changes sign.
must_cover: Let \(h(x)=f(x)g(x)\), where \(f\) is even and \(g\) is odd.
Then \(h(-x)=f(-x)g(-x)\).
So \(h(-x)=-f(x)g(x)=-h(x)\), which means \(h\) is odd.
The graph example \(x^2\cdot x^3=x^5\) confirms the rule visually.
slides.transition_from_previous: Piecewise examples were computational; this challenge is a definition-based reasoning check.
slides.transition_to_next: Now compress the whole lecture into the checklist students should keep.
slides.likely_student_confusion: Students may memorize product rules without proof. Keep the definitions visible and let them do the work.
slides.explanation_style: Use short text chunks as anchors, but teach by moving the visual state and pointing to one concrete formula or graph feature.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should see the graph or formula change at the exact moment the spoken explanation names the rule.
slides.scene_hint: Use the graph confirmation after the proof: even curve, odd curve, then product curve changing sign.

slides.narration_seed: Close by tying each takeaway back to one slide: families, domain and range, transformations, composition, symmetry, and piecewise boundaries.
must_cover: Recognize function families by formula and graph shape.
Check domain and range before doing operations.
Apply transformations in order and beware the horizontal-sign trap.
For composition, apply the inside function first and keep both domain restrictions.
Use symmetry and piecewise boundary behavior as graph-reading tools for calculus.
slides.transition_from_previous: The challenge proved one rule from definitions; the summary shows how all rules fit together.
slides.transition_to_next: Next lesson can start using this graph language for limits and rates of change.
slides.likely_student_confusion: Students may leave with separate facts instead of one toolkit. Frame the recap as a toolbox for limits and derivatives.
slides.explanation_style: Use the visible chunks as anchors, then explain in plain language with one concrete graph or formula reference.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should know what to look at, what rule is being used, and why the rule matters.
